summary: McKenzie reflects on the difficulty of maintaining a side project development schedule while working a demanding day job with long hours and a brutal commute. He describes an intellectually satisfying day at work where he wrote 800 lines of elegant code solving a complex constraint satisfaction problem, comparing it to a zoo exhibit placement puzzle. Despite making no progress on his own projects, he finds satisfaction in the craftsmanship of his day job work, while acknowledging the tension between employment demands and entrepreneurial ambitions.
key_points: A demanding day job with long hours and commute leaves little energy for side project development
Complex constraint satisfaction problems are among the rare cases where a CS degree's theoretical foundations prove directly useful
The intrinsic satisfaction of solving hard technical problems can keep you working late even when it's not your own business
Having 600 automated tests pass on elegant code is a deeply rewarding engineering experience
money_quotes: This was much easier last time, when I was at the job which got out at 4:30 and has a 20 minute commute, rather than the one that got out at 9:00 with the 2 hour commute…
I did manage to put some of the finishing touches on the best 800 lines of code I've ever written.
Sadly the day job owns it so I can't go around showing it off, but darn, when all six hundred automated tests worked it was a beautiful, beautiful thing.
Its probably one of the few times where I actually got to put my CS degree qua CS degree to use, rather than just using programming skills.
I stayed a little later than I was planning not so much because this was urgent to get done today but because I was so close to beating the stupid thing.
key_insight: The tension between a demanding day job and side project ambitions is real, and the intellectual seduction of hard engineering problems at work can further delay entrepreneurial progress.
